rationale: PNPLA3 rs738409 CC (wild-type, Ile148Ile) confers standard hepatic lipid metabolism with no increased steatosis risk. Standard ALT reference range of 7-35 U/L applies. No genotype-based adjustment is required. This serves as the baseline genotype for comparison with CG and GG carriers.
text_chunk: PNPLA3 rs738409 CC (wild-type) genotype: standard ALT reference range 7-35 U/L. The CC genotype at PNPLA3 rs738409 encodes the Ile148 wild-type protein with normal lipid droplet remodeling activity. These individuals have baseline hepatic fat content and standard ALT levels. No adjustment to the reference range is needed. PNPLA3 (patatin-like phospholipase domain-containing protein 3) is expressed on hepatic lipid droplets and catalyzes triglyceride hydrolysis. The wild-type enzyme maintains normal hepatic lipid turnover.

rationale: PNPLA3 rs738409 CG (heterozygous, Ile148Met carrier) confers modestly increased hepatic fat accumulation due to partial loss of lipid droplet remodeling function. ALT may be mildly elevated at baseline (3-5 U/L above population mean) without indicating liver pathology. Upper reference limit adjusted to 40 U/L to reduce false-positive NAFLD screening in carriers. Clinical correlation with imaging (FibroScan, ultrasound) recommended if ALT exceeds 40 U/L.
text_chunk: PNPLA3 rs738409 CG (heterozygous) genotype: adjusted ALT reference range 7-40 U/L. The CG genotype carries one copy of the Met148 variant, partially impairing PNPLA3-mediated triglyceride hydrolysis on hepatic lipid droplets. This leads to modest hepatic steatosis with ALT levels approximately 3-5 U/L above population mean. Adjusting the upper limit to 40 U/L prevents over-investigation of genotype-related baseline elevation while maintaining sensitivity for true hepatic injury. NAFLD prevalence is approximately 35% in CG carriers versus 25% in CC.

rationale: PNPLA3 rs738409 GG (homozygous Met148Met) causes major loss of hepatic lipid droplet remodeling, with 73% increased hepatic fat content versus CC. Approximately 35% of GG carriers need upper limit extension to 45 U/L because their baseline ALT is elevated due to chronic hepatic lipid accumulation rather than active hepatocellular injury. ALT values 35-45 U/L in GG carriers warrant monitoring (FIB-4 score, imaging) but do not necessarily indicate progressive liver disease. Values above 45 U/L should trigger standard hepatological evaluation.
text_chunk: PNPLA3 rs738409 GG (homozygous) genotype: adjusted ALT reference range 7-45 U/L. The GG genotype (Met148Met) severely impairs PNPLA3 lipase activity, causing 73% increased hepatic fat content and 3.2-fold increased NAFLD risk. Approximately 35% of GG carriers have baseline ALT elevation above 35 U/L due to chronic hepatic steatosis rather than active hepatocellular damage. Adjusting the upper limit to 45 U/L prevents unnecessary liver biopsies while maintaining vigilance: ALT above 45 U/L in GG carriers warrants FIB-4 scoring, transient elastography, and hepatology referral. GG carriers have 3.2x NASH risk and 5.0x cirrhosis risk.

rationale: TCF7L2 rs7903146 CC (wild-type) confers standard type 2 diabetes risk. The standard fasting glucose reference range of 70-99 mg/dL applies without adjustment. TCF7L2 encodes transcription factor 7-like 2, a key Wnt signaling effector regulating pancreatic beta-cell proliferation and incretin hormone (GLP-1) processing. CC carriers have normal beta-cell function and insulin secretion dynamics.
text_chunk: TCF7L2 rs7903146 CC (wild-type) genotype: standard fasting glucose reference range 70-99 mg/dL. The CC genotype at TCF7L2 rs7903146 is the wild-type with normal Wnt signaling in pancreatic beta-cells. TCF7L2 regulates proglucagon gene expression in intestinal L-cells (affecting GLP-1 production) and beta-cell proliferation/survival. CC carriers have normal incretin effect and insulin secretory capacity. Standard ADA fasting glucose criteria apply: normal <100, pre-diabetes 100-125, diabetes >=126 mg/dL.

rationale: TCF7L2 rs7903146 CT (heterozygous) confers approximately 1.3-fold increased type 2 diabetes risk due to impaired incretin signaling and reduced beta-cell compensation. The adjusted upper glucose threshold of 95 mg/dL triggers earlier monitoring because CT carriers progress from normal to pre-diabetes faster than CC individuals. Fasting glucose values 95-99 mg/dL in CT carriers should prompt oral glucose tolerance testing (OGTT) and HbA1c assessment.
text_chunk: TCF7L2 rs7903146 CT (heterozygous) genotype: adjusted fasting glucose range 70-95 mg/dL with tighter monitoring threshold. CT carriers have 1.3-fold increased T2D risk due to impaired GLP-1 secretion from intestinal L-cells and reduced beta-cell proliferative response to glucose stimulation. While standard ADA criteria define pre-diabetes at 100 mg/dL, CT carriers benefit from earlier intervention when fasting glucose reaches 95 mg/dL. At this threshold, OGTT and HbA1c testing should be performed to detect early dysglycemia before it meets standard diagnostic criteria.

rationale: TCF7L2 rs7903146 TT (homozygous risk) confers 1.4-fold increased type 2 diabetes risk, the strongest single-gene T2D risk variant identified by GWAS. The TT genotype significantly impairs beta-cell function, GLP-1 secretion, and hepatic glucose regulation. An aggressive monitoring threshold of 90 mg/dL is recommended because TT carriers transition from normal glycemia to frank diabetes more rapidly and with less metabolic reserve. Values 90-99 mg/dL should trigger comprehensive metabolic assessment including OGTT, fasting insulin, HOMA-IR, and HbA1c.
text_chunk: TCF7L2 rs7903146 TT (homozygous risk) genotype: adjusted fasting glucose range 70-90 mg/dL with aggressive monitoring. TT carriers have 1.4-fold increased T2D risk, the strongest common genetic risk factor for type 2 diabetes identified in GWAS meta-analyses. The TT genotype impairs beta-cell mass expansion, GLP-1-mediated insulin secretion (incretin effect reduced by 30%), and hepatic glucose output regulation. Fasting glucose above 90 mg/dL in TT carriers should trigger comprehensive metabolic evaluation: OGTT, fasting insulin, HOMA-IR calculation, and HbA1c. Lifestyle intervention at this threshold can reduce diabetes conversion by 58% (DPP trial).

rationale: TCF7L2 rs7903146 TT carriers have the highest genetic risk for T2D and benefit from a tighter HbA1c optimal threshold of <5.4% versus the standard <5.7% pre-diabetes cutoff. TT carriers with HbA1c 5.4-5.6% are already showing glycemic drift toward diabetes and should be considered for intensive lifestyle modification and metabolic monitoring. The reduced threshold provides a 2-3 year earlier intervention window before reaching the standard 5.7% pre-diabetes diagnostic threshold.
text_chunk: TCF7L2 rs7903146 TT genotype: adjusted optimal HbA1c threshold <5.4% (versus standard <5.7%). The TT genotype confers the highest common genetic T2D risk, and HbA1c values 5.4-5.6% in these individuals represent a critical intervention window. While ADA defines pre-diabetes at HbA1c 5.7%, TT carriers at 5.4% are already showing glycemic trajectory toward diabetes due to impaired incretin effect and reduced beta-cell compensatory capacity. Early intervention at 5.4% provides a 2-3 year lead time for lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight management) before standard diagnostic thresholds are met. This genotype-adjusted threshold enables precision prevention.

rationale: APOE E3/E3 is the most common genotype (60-70% of population) and represents the standard reference for LDL cholesterol metabolism. The standard LDL target of <130 mg/dL applies for primary prevention in individuals without additional cardiovascular risk factors. E3/E3 carriers have normal LDL receptor binding affinity and standard cholesterol clearance kinetics. Standard ASCVD risk assessment (Pooled Cohort Equations) applies without genetic modification.
text_chunk: APOE E3/E3 genotype: standard LDL cholesterol target <130 mg/dL for primary prevention. E3/E3 is the most common APOE genotype (60-70% prevalence) with normal apolipoprotein E function. ApoE3 binds LDL receptors with normal affinity, maintaining standard cholesterol clearance. Standard ACC/AHA ASCVD risk assessment applies. LDL targets should be further adjusted based on 10-year ASCVD risk: <130 for low risk, <100 for intermediate, <70 for high risk or established CVD. No genotype-specific cardiovascular or Alzheimer disease risk modification is needed.

rationale: APOE E3/E4 carriers have increased LDL cholesterol absorption and reduced LDL receptor expression. This genotype confers 2-3x increased Alzheimer disease risk and 1.3x increased cardiovascular risk. The adjusted LDL target of <100 mg/dL reflects the need for more aggressive lipid management due to the combined cardiovascular and neurological risk. ApoE4 enhances cholesterol delivery to the brain but impairs amyloid-beta clearance, creating a dual indication for LDL reduction.
text_chunk: APOE E3/E4 genotype: adjusted LDL cholesterol target <100 mg/dL. E3/E4 carriers have approximately 20% higher LDL cholesterol than E3/E3 individuals due to enhanced intestinal cholesterol absorption and relatively reduced hepatic LDL receptor expression. E4 carriers face 2-3x increased Alzheimer disease risk (via impaired amyloid-beta clearance) and 1.3x increased cardiovascular risk. The tighter LDL target of <100 mg/dL addresses both vascular and neurological risk. Statins and PCSK9 inhibitors effectively lower LDL in E4 carriers. Dietary cholesterol restriction has greater impact in E4 carriers due to enhanced absorption sensitivity.

rationale: APOE E4/E4 homozygosity confers the highest genetic risk for both late-onset Alzheimer disease (8-12x increased risk) and cardiovascular disease. The aggressive LDL target of <70 mg/dL matches the secondary prevention target and reflects the extremely high lifetime cardiovascular and neurodegenerative risk. ApoE4/E4 individuals have the highest LDL cholesterol, most efficient cholesterol absorption, and poorest amyloid-beta clearance. This genotype effectively categorizes individuals as high-risk equivalent for lipid management purposes regardless of other risk factors.
text_chunk: APOE E4/E4 genotype: adjusted LDL cholesterol target <70 mg/dL. E4/E4 homozygosity confers 8-12x increased Alzheimer disease risk and significantly elevated cardiovascular risk. These individuals have the highest LDL cholesterol levels among APOE genotypes, enhanced dietary cholesterol absorption, and impaired hepatic remnant lipoprotein clearance. The LDL target of <70 mg/dL matches secondary prevention goals and is justified by the extremely high combined cardiovascular and neurodegenerative risk. High-intensity statin therapy and/or PCSK9 inhibitors are often required. Dietary modifications (plant sterol-enriched diet, reduced saturated fat) are particularly impactful in E4/E4 carriers.

rationale: DIO2 rs225014 CC (wild-type, Thr92Thr) encodes normal type 2 deiodinase activity, enabling efficient intracellular conversion of T4 to T3 in the brain, pituitary, thyroid, and skeletal muscle. Standard TSH reference range of 0.45-4.5 mIU/L applies. Standard Free T3 range of 2.0-4.4 pg/mL applies. No adjustment to thyroid function interpretation is needed for CC carriers.
text_chunk: DIO2 rs225014 CC (wild-type) genotype: standard TSH reference range 0.45-4.5 mIU/L and Free T3 2.0-4.4 pg/mL. The CC genotype encodes Thr92Thr (wild-type) type 2 deiodinase (DIO2), the primary enzyme responsible for intracellular T4-to-T3 conversion in the brain, pituitary, and skeletal muscle. Normal DIO2 activity ensures adequate intracellular T3 availability when serum T4 is in the normal range. Standard levothyroxine (T4-only) replacement is fully effective in CC carriers because they efficiently convert exogenous T4 to T3 at the tissue level.

rationale: DIO2 rs225014 CT (heterozygous, Thr92Ala carrier) has mildly impaired T4-to-T3 conversion. These individuals may have slightly higher TSH requirements to maintain intracellular T3 levels, and some may report persistent hypothyroid symptoms despite normal TSH on T4-only therapy. A slightly tighter TSH upper limit of 4.0 mIU/L may be appropriate, particularly in patients with residual symptoms on levothyroxine. Some CT carriers benefit from combination T4/T3 therapy.
text_chunk: DIO2 rs225014 CT (heterozygous) genotype: TSH monitoring with awareness of impaired T4-to-T3 conversion. CT carriers have mildly reduced DIO2 activity (Thr92Ala heterozygous), which may impair intracellular T3 generation particularly in the brain and skeletal muscle. While serum TSH and T4 may appear normal, tissue-level T3 may be insufficient. Some CT carriers on levothyroxine replacement report persistent fatigue, cognitive fog, and weight gain despite normal TSH. A slightly tighter TSH upper target of 4.0 mIU/L and attention to Free T3 levels may be warranted. Combination T4/T3 therapy should be considered if symptoms persist.

rationale: DIO2 rs225014 TT (homozygous Ala92Ala) significantly impairs intracellular T4-to-T3 conversion, with approximately 25% reduced deiodinase activity. These individuals may have adequate serum T4 and TSH but insufficient tissue T3, particularly in brain and muscle. Adjusted TSH target of 0.5-3.0 mIU/L and a preference for Free T3 >3.0 pg/mL are recommended. TT carriers are more likely to benefit from combination T4/T3 therapy (liothyronine or desiccated thyroid). The ETA-2012 trial suggested TT carriers had improved psychological well-being on combination T4/T3 versus T4-only.
text_chunk: DIO2 rs225014 TT (homozygous) genotype: adjusted TSH range 0.5-3.0 mIU/L, may need T3 supplementation. TT carriers (Ala92Ala) have approximately 25% reduced DIO2 deiodinase activity, significantly impairing intracellular T4-to-T3 conversion. This creates a disconnect between serum markers (normal TSH, normal T4) and tissue T3 levels (deficient in brain, muscle). TT carriers on levothyroxine monotherapy frequently report residual hypothyroid symptoms. The adjusted TSH target of 0.5-3.0 mIU/L aims to ensure higher circulating T4 substrate for impaired conversion. Combination T4/T3 therapy should be strongly considered, supported by the Panicker et al. pharmacogenomic study demonstrating improved outcomes in TT carriers on combination therapy.

rationale: DIO2 rs225014 TT carriers have impaired T4-to-T3 conversion, making Free T3 a critical monitoring parameter. The adjusted optimal Free T3 target of >3.0 pg/mL (versus standard lower limit of 2.0 pg/mL) ensures adequate tissue T3 delivery despite reduced deiodinase activity. TT carriers with Free T3 below 3.0 pg/mL are likely experiencing tissue-level T3 deficiency even if TSH is normal. Monitoring Free T3 is more informative than Total T3 in these patients. Combination T4/T3 therapy typically achieves this target more reliably than T4 monotherapy.
text_chunk: DIO2 rs225014 TT genotype: adjusted optimal Free T3 target >3.0 pg/mL (versus standard lower limit 2.0 pg/mL). TT carriers with impaired DIO2 activity cannot efficiently generate intracellular T3 from T4, making circulating Free T3 a more clinically relevant marker than TSH for assessing thyroid adequacy. Free T3 below 3.0 pg/mL in TT carriers suggests tissue-level T3 deficiency and correlates with persistent hypothyroid symptoms (fatigue, depression, cognitive impairment, cold intolerance, weight gain). Treatment goal is Free T3 in the upper half of the reference range (3.0-4.4 pg/mL) using combination T4/T3 therapy or optimized T4 monotherapy with TSH suppression to the lower normal range.

rationale: HFE rs1800562 GG (wild-type, Cys282Cys) confers normal iron metabolism with appropriate hepcidin regulation. Standard ferritin reference ranges apply: 20-300 ng/mL for males and 12-150 ng/mL for females. No genotype-based adjustment is needed. Ferritin should be interpreted in context of inflammation (ferritin is an acute-phase reactant; elevations may reflect inflammation rather than iron overload). Normal transferrin saturation (<45%) confirms appropriate iron status.
text_chunk: HFE rs1800562 GG (wild-type) genotype: standard ferritin reference range 20-300 ng/mL (males), 12-150 ng/mL (females). The GG genotype at HFE C282Y encodes normal HFE protein function, maintaining appropriate hepcidin signaling and ferroportin-mediated iron export regulation. Standard ferritin ranges apply. Ferritin is both an iron storage protein and acute-phase reactant, so elevations should be interpreted alongside transferrin saturation, CRP, and liver function tests to distinguish iron overload from inflammatory elevation.

rationale: HFE rs1800562 GA (C282Y heterozygous carrier) has mildly impaired hepcidin regulation, with potential for gradual iron accumulation over decades. While clinical hemochromatosis penetrance is less than 1% in heterozygotes, a lower ferritin upper alert threshold of 200 ng/mL (males) and 100 ng/mL (females) enables earlier detection of iron trending. Transferrin saturation should be monitored alongside ferritin. Values approaching the adjusted upper limit warrant annual monitoring and dietary counseling regarding iron intake.
text_chunk: HFE rs1800562 GA (C282Y heterozygous carrier) genotype: adjusted upper ferritin limit 200 ng/mL (males), 100 ng/mL (females). Heterozygous C282Y carriers have mildly reduced hepcidin-mediated iron regulation with <1% penetrance for clinical hemochromatosis. However, iron accumulation can occur gradually, especially with dietary iron excess, alcohol use, or co-existing metabolic syndrome. The lowered ferritin alert threshold enables proactive monitoring before clinical iron overload develops. Annual ferritin and transferrin saturation monitoring recommended. Dietary guidance: moderate heme iron intake, avoid iron-fortified supplements.

rationale: HFE rs1800562 AA (C282Y homozygous) causes hereditary hemochromatosis type 1 with progressive iron overload. The therapeutic target ferritin of <50 ng/mL is the established treatment goal, maintained through phlebotomy. Ferritin above 50 ng/mL in homozygotes indicates iron accumulation requiring therapeutic intervention. Transferrin saturation >45% confirms active iron loading. Untreated iron overload leads to liver cirrhosis (7x risk), hepatocellular carcinoma (20x risk), cardiomyopathy, diabetes (bronze diabetes), arthropathy, and hypogonadism.
text_chunk: HFE rs1800562 AA (C282Y homozygous) genotype: therapeutic ferritin target <50 ng/mL. C282Y homozygosity causes hereditary hemochromatosis type 1 (HH1) with severely dysregulated hepcidin production, leading to uncontrolled ferroportin-mediated iron absorption. Without intervention, iron overload progresses to hepatic fibrosis/cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma (20x risk), dilated cardiomyopathy, pancreatic beta-cell destruction (bronze diabetes), destructive arthropathy, and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Therapeutic phlebotomy maintains ferritin below 50 ng/mL and transferrin saturation below 50%. Iron supplements are absolutely contraindicated. Penetrance varies: 28-38% of male and 1-14% of female homozygotes develop clinical disease.

rationale: FADS1 rs174547 CC (normal converter) has standard delta-5 desaturase activity, converting ALA to EPA at approximately 5-10% efficiency. The standard optimal omega-3 index target of >8% is achievable through both dietary ALA sources and preformed EPA/DHA. CC carriers respond well to plant-based omega-3 supplementation (flaxseed, chia) and can achieve optimal omega-3 status with modest fish oil supplementation (1 g/day EPA+DHA).
text_chunk: FADS1 rs174547 CC (normal converter) genotype: standard optimal omega-3 index target >8%. CC carriers have normal FADS1 (delta-5 desaturase) enzyme activity, enabling standard ALA-to-EPA conversion at approximately 5-10% efficiency. Both plant-based ALA sources (flaxseed, walnuts, chia seeds) and preformed EPA/DHA (fish, fish oil, algal oil) effectively contribute to omega-3 status. Standard 1 g/day EPA+DHA supplementation typically achieves the 8% omega-3 index target associated with 30% reduced cardiac death risk in the Framingham Heart Study.

rationale: FADS1 rs174547 CT (intermediate converter) has 30-40% reduced delta-5 desaturase activity. An adjusted acceptable omega-3 index threshold of >6% with ALA supplementation reflects the reduced conversion capacity, though >8% remains the ideal target achievable with preformed EPA/DHA. CT carriers benefit from combining ALA-rich foods with moderate EPA/DHA supplementation (1-2 g/day). Semi-annual omega-3 index testing recommended to verify adequacy.
text_chunk: FADS1 rs174547 CT (intermediate converter) genotype: adjusted acceptable omega-3 index >6% on ALA supplementation, optimal target remains >8% with preformed EPA/DHA. CT carriers have 30-40% reduced FADS1 delta-5 desaturase activity, partially impairing ALA-to-EPA conversion. On ALA-only supplementation, an omega-3 index of 6% may be the practical achievable limit and is acceptable. However, preformed EPA/DHA supplementation at 1-2 g/day can still achieve the optimal 8% target. Combined ALA dietary sources plus moderate fish oil supplementation is the recommended approach. Semi-annual omega-3 index testing recommended.

rationale: FADS1 rs174547 TT (poor converter) has 60-70% reduced delta-5 desaturase activity. On ALA supplementation alone, omega-3 index rarely exceeds 5%, which is the adjusted acceptable minimum for this genotype. Preformed EPA/DHA at 2-4 g/day is essential to achieve adequate omega-3 status. These individuals effectively cannot rely on plant-based omega-3 sources. The adjusted minimum of 5% reflects the biological limitation rather than a clinical recommendation; the true optimal target remains >8% achievable only with preformed EPA/DHA.
text_chunk: FADS1 rs174547 TT (poor converter) genotype: requires preformed EPA/DHA, adjusted omega-3 index minimum >5% on ALA alone, optimal >8% with preformed EPA/DHA 2-4 g/day. TT carriers have 60-70% reduced FADS1 delta-5 desaturase activity, making endogenous ALA-to-EPA/DHA conversion negligible. Plant-based omega-3 sources (flaxseed, chia, walnuts) are essentially ineffective for achieving adequate omega-3 status in TT carriers. Preformed EPA/DHA from marine sources (fish oil, algal oil) at 2-4 g/day is mandatory. Even with supplementation, TT carriers may require higher doses to achieve the 8% omega-3 index target. Quarterly omega-3 index monitoring recommended during dose optimization.

rationale: MTHFR rs1801133 CC (wild-type, Ala222Ala) has normal MTHFR enzyme activity with full capacity for 5-MTHF production and homocysteine remethylation. The standard homocysteine upper limit of 10 umol/L applies. Values above 10 umol/L in CC carriers more likely indicate B12/folate deficiency, renal impairment, hypothyroidism, or medication effects rather than a genetic methylation defect.
text_chunk: MTHFR rs1801133 CC (wild-type) genotype: standard homocysteine reference <10 umol/L. CC carriers have normal MTHFR enzyme activity (100% thermostable enzyme function) and efficiently convert 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate to 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF), the primary circulating folate form and methyl donor for homocysteine remethylation via methionine synthase. Standard folic acid supplementation effectively supports methylation cycle function. Homocysteine elevation in CC carriers should prompt investigation for B12 deficiency, renal dysfunction, hypothyroidism, or B6 deficiency rather than MTHFR impairment.

rationale: MTHFR rs1801133 CT (heterozygous, Ala222Val carrier) has approximately 35% reduced MTHFR enzyme activity, causing mildly impaired homocysteine remethylation. The adjusted alert threshold of 12 umol/L accounts for the genotype-related tendency toward mildly elevated homocysteine. Values between 10-12 umol/L in CT carriers may reflect the genetic variant rather than pathological methylation impairment, but should still prompt methylfolate supplementation (400-800 mcg/day) and B12 assessment.
text_chunk: MTHFR rs1801133 CT (heterozygous) genotype: adjusted homocysteine alert threshold <12 umol/L. CT carriers have approximately 35% reduced MTHFR enzyme activity due to the thermolabile Ala222Val variant. This mildly impairs 5-MTHF production, causing a tendency toward homocysteine elevation of 1-3 umol/L above the CC mean. The adjusted threshold of 12 umol/L prevents unnecessary alarm at mildly elevated levels while maintaining clinical vigilance. CT carriers with homocysteine 10-12 umol/L should receive methylfolate 400-800 mcg/day, methylcobalamin 500-1000 mcg/day, and dietary folate counseling. Recheck homocysteine after 3 months of supplementation.

rationale: MTHFR rs1801133 TT (homozygous, Val222Val) has approximately 70% reduced MTHFR enzyme activity, causing significant impairment of homocysteine remethylation. Homocysteine levels of 12-15 umol/L are commonly observed in TT carriers without supplementation. The adjusted alert threshold of 15 umol/L recognizes the higher genotype-driven baseline while maintaining urgency for values above 15 umol/L (associated with cardiovascular disease, stroke, and pregnancy complications). Mandatory supplementation with methylfolate 800-1000 mcg/day, methylcobalamin, and P5P is recommended for all TT carriers.
text_chunk: MTHFR rs1801133 TT (homozygous) genotype: adjusted homocysteine alert threshold <15 umol/L, mandatory methylfolate supplementation. TT carriers have approximately 70% reduced MTHFR enzyme activity, severely impairing 5-MTHF production and homocysteine remethylation. Unsupplemented TT carriers frequently have homocysteine levels of 12-20 umol/L. While levels up to 15 umol/L represent the expected genotype-driven range with basic supplementation, values above 15 umol/L indicate inadequate methylation support and require aggressive intervention: methylfolate 800-1000 mcg, methylcobalamin 1000 mcg, P5P 50 mg, and potentially TMG 1000-3000 mg daily. Target homocysteine below 8 umol/L for optimal cardiovascular protection.

rationale: GCKR rs1260326 CC (wild-type, Pro446Pro) encodes normal glucokinase regulatory protein function, maintaining standard hepatic glucose metabolism and triglyceride production. The standard triglyceride threshold of <150 mg/dL applies. GCKR modulates glucokinase activity in the liver, regulating glucose uptake and de novo lipogenesis. CC carriers have baseline triglyceride metabolism without genotype-driven elevation.
text_chunk: GCKR rs1260326 CC (wild-type) genotype: standard triglyceride reference <150 mg/dL. The CC genotype encodes normal GCKR (glucokinase regulatory protein) function. GCKR inhibits hepatic glucokinase in the fasting state, preventing excessive glucose phosphorylation. Wild-type GCKR maintains appropriate regulation of hepatic glucose uptake and downstream de novo lipogenesis. Standard AHA/ACC triglyceride targets apply: normal <150, borderline 150-199, high 200-499, very high >=500 mg/dL.

rationale: GCKR rs1260326 CT (heterozygous, Pro446Leu carrier) partially reduces GCKR inhibitory function, allowing increased glucokinase activity. This enhances hepatic glucose uptake and de novo lipogenesis, resulting in mildly elevated triglycerides (approximately 5-10 mg/dL above CC mean). The adjusted awareness threshold of <130 mg/dL triggers earlier metabolic evaluation and lifestyle intervention. Paradoxically, CT carriers have lower fasting glucose (beneficial) but higher triglycerides and increased NAFLD risk.
text_chunk: GCKR rs1260326 CT (heterozygous) genotype: adjusted triglyceride awareness threshold <130 mg/dL. CT carriers have partially reduced GCKR inhibitory function, increasing hepatic glucokinase activity. This paradoxically lowers fasting glucose (beneficial, lower T2D risk) while increasing de novo lipogenesis and triglyceride production. The adjusted threshold of 130 mg/dL triggers earlier dietary modification (reduced refined carbohydrates, increased omega-3 intake) and metabolic monitoring. CT carriers also have modestly increased NAFLD risk through enhanced hepatic lipogenesis.

rationale: GCKR rs1260326 TT (homozygous Leu446Leu) substantially reduces GCKR inhibition of glucokinase, causing constitutively increased hepatic glucose uptake and de novo lipogenesis. TT carriers have approximately 15-25 mg/dL higher triglycerides than CC at baseline. The adjusted optimal threshold of <120 mg/dL reflects the need for tighter triglyceride management to compensate for the genetic predisposition. Despite having lower fasting glucose and reduced T2D risk, TT carriers have increased triglycerides, NAFLD, and cardiovascular risk requiring proactive lipid management.
text_chunk: GCKR rs1260326 TT (homozygous) genotype: adjusted optimal triglyceride threshold <120 mg/dL. TT carriers (Leu446Leu) have substantially reduced GCKR-mediated glucokinase inhibition, leading to constitutively enhanced hepatic glucose phosphorylation and de novo lipogenesis. This produces 15-25 mg/dL higher baseline triglycerides. The adjusted threshold of <120 mg/dL enables proactive cardiovascular risk management. Interventions: restrict refined carbohydrates and fructose (substrate for hepatic DNL), increase omega-3 intake (EPA 1.8-4 g/day), and consider fibrates if triglycerides exceed 150 mg/dL despite lifestyle modification.

rationale: LPA rs10455872 AA (wild-type) is associated with standard Lp(a) levels. The standard Lp(a) desirable range of <30 mg/dL (or <75 nmol/L) applies. Lp(a) is a genetically determined lipoprotein consisting of an LDL-like particle with apolipoprotein(a) covalently bound to apoB100. Lp(a) levels are 80-90% genetically determined and minimally responsive to lifestyle modification. AA carriers at rs10455872 have standard Lp(a) levels.
text_chunk: LPA rs10455872 AA (wild-type) genotype: standard Lp(a) reference <30 mg/dL (75 nmol/L). The AA genotype is associated with normal kringle IV-2 repeat number and standard Lp(a) plasma levels. Lp(a) is a causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, aortic stenosis, and ischemic stroke, with risk beginning at levels above 30 mg/dL. Lp(a) is 80-90% genetically determined and does not respond to diet, exercise, or statin therapy. PCSK9 inhibitors reduce Lp(a) by 20-30%. Novel antisense oligonucleotides (olpasiran, pelacarsen) targeting LPA mRNA are in Phase 3 clinical trials.

rationale: LPA rs10455872 AG or GG genotypes are associated with genetically elevated Lp(a), typically 2-5x above population median. Since Lp(a) is 80-90% genetically determined, these individuals have lifelong elevated cardiovascular risk that cannot be addressed through standard lifestyle modification. Therapeutic targets focus on maximizing modifiable risk factor control: aggressive LDL reduction (<70 mg/dL), PCSK9 inhibitor therapy (reduces Lp(a) by 20-30%), aspirin consideration, and enrollment in Lp(a)-lowering clinical trials (olpasiran, pelacarsen).
text_chunk: LPA rs10455872 AG/GG genotype: genetically elevated Lp(a), therapeutic approach required. AG and GG carriers have genetically elevated Lp(a) levels typically 50-200+ mg/dL (120-500+ nmol/L), conferring significantly increased risk for ASCVD, aortic valve stenosis, and ischemic stroke. Since Lp(a) is 80-90% genetically determined, lifestyle interventions have minimal impact. Management strategy: (1) aggressive LDL reduction to <70 mg/dL using high-intensity statin + ezetimibe + PCSK9i, (2) PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) reduce Lp(a) by 20-30%, (3) consider aspirin for primary prevention, (4) niacin reduces Lp(a) by 20-30% but lacks outcomes evidence, (5) emerging therapies: pelacarsen and olpasiran (antisense/siRNA targeting hepatic LPA mRNA) reduce Lp(a) by 80-98% in Phase 2/3 trials.

rationale: VDR rs2228570 CC (wild-type, FokI FF genotype) produces the shorter, more transcriptionally active vitamin D receptor. Standard vitamin D supplementation (1000-2000 IU/day) typically achieves the optimal range of 30-80 ng/mL. The full-length receptor binds 1,25(OH)2D with normal affinity and effectively transactivates vitamin D-responsive genes (CYP24A1, osteocalcin, cathelicidin, TRPV6). No dose adjustment is needed.
text_chunk: VDR rs2228570 CC (wild-type, FokI FF) genotype: standard optimal vitamin D range 30-80 ng/mL. The CC genotype produces the shorter VDR protein (424 amino acids), which has 1.7-fold greater transcriptional activity than the longer form. This more active receptor efficiently transduces the vitamin D signal, enabling standard supplementation doses (1000-2000 IU/day) to achieve biological effects at typical serum 25(OH)D concentrations. Vitamin D target of 30-50 ng/mL is adequate for calcium homeostasis, bone health, immune modulation, and cancer risk reduction in CC carriers.

rationale: VDR rs2228570 TT (FokI ff genotype) produces the longer, less transcriptionally active vitamin D receptor (427 amino acids). This receptor has reduced transcriptional efficiency, requiring higher circulating 25(OH)D to achieve equivalent vitamin D-responsive gene expression. The adjusted optimal range of 40-80 ng/mL ensures adequate downstream signaling despite reduced receptor activity. Higher supplementation doses (4000-5000 IU/day) are typically needed to achieve levels above 40 ng/mL. TT carriers may appear vitamin D sufficient by standard criteria (>30 ng/mL) while being functionally deficient.
text_chunk: VDR rs2228570 TT (FokI ff) genotype: adjusted optimal vitamin D range 40-80 ng/mL, higher supplementation needed. The TT genotype produces the longer VDR protein (427 amino acids), which has approximately 40% less transcriptional activity than the shorter CC form. This means TT carriers need higher circulating 25(OH)D concentrations to achieve the same biological effects (calcium absorption, antimicrobial peptide production, immune modulation). Supplementation of 4000-5000 IU/day is typically required. TT carriers with 25(OH)D of 30-39 ng/mL may be functionally vitamin D insufficient despite meeting standard sufficiency criteria. This genotype-adjusted range enables precision vitamin D management.
